WRITEUP
CAPESIZE
The market weakened further today, with the BCI 182 5TC slipping by $1,416 to settle at $24,682, reflecting a continuation of the softer tone seen this week. In the Pacific, two miners were back in the market, but despite what has been a relatively busy week in terms of enquiry, conditions remained largely flat. Fixtures on C5 were reported in the $7.80–$8.00 range, with the C5 index easing marginally by 0.110 to close at $7.880. In the Atlantic, sentiment deteriorated further as activity from South Brazil and West Africa to China remained limited. On C3, bids for end-January/early-February laycan were said to have slipped to $20.00–$20.50, versus offers around $21.50. A Seven Islands to China fixture concluded yesterday weighed on the market, with the C9 index losing $2,611 to settle at $49,167, while the C8 index also dropped $2,613 to $30,325 with talk of a potential C7 fixture at around $13.95.
Atlantic
Glencore fixed yesterday Saiko (180,178 2010) for 170,000/10 from Seven Islands to Qingdao for 22/28 January at $28.80.
Asia
Rio Tinto fixed a TBN for 170,000/10 from Dampier to Qingdao for 24/26 January at $7.80. BHP fixed a TBN for 160,000/10 from Port Hedland to Qingdao for 18/20 January at $8.00 and a second TBN for 19/21 January at $7.90, further details have not come to light.
PANAMAX
Trans-Atlantic activity continues to be relatively stable with the near term available tonnage clearing, while the fronthaul market is also showing greater resilience and providing a firmer underpinning to Atlantic rates. Enquiry for end January and early February positions is beginning to build, which should also help absorb available tonnage. In the Pacific, Indonesian demand is gaining momentum and brokers reporting firmer rate ideas being circulated, particularly for modern vessels that are now considering longer Pacific employment or positioning towards EC South America. The P5TC average posted a $177 increase to publish at $12,028.
Atlantic
Today it was reported that the Prevail Star (81,055 2014) open in Montoir 7 January failed with Refined Success for a trip via NC South America option US Gulf redelivery China at $18,000. Several vessels, including Darya Ruchi (82,557 2022), Yangze 16 (82,027 2019) and Intuition (80,327 201), were placed on subjects for EC South America fronthaul trades from the Indian subcontinent and SE Asia, with details largely undisclosed. The Panasiatic (82,962 2005) was reported open EC South America mid January was also placed on subjects, again further details were not disclosed.
Asia
In the Pacific, activity included Northwind (82,308 2020) fixing ex Laem Chabang for Indonesia/Philippines. Also the MSXT Echo (85,187 2021) fixed ex Guishan 8 January to Tongli, and the scrubber fitted Swift Hangzhou (79,343 2015) fixed ex Yeosu to Pan Ocean, all with rates undisclosed.
SUPRAMAX
The downward trend continued today with little sign in any change for the owning side. The Atlantic remained in the doldrums despite reasonable levels of fresh enquiry from the US Gulf although there remained a good amount of prompt tonnage. The Continent/Mediterranean remained rather positional although again rates did not improve. Limited activity from the South Atlantic, the Eva Linden (63,614 2024) open Santos 18/20 January was heard to have been fixed on voyage terms for a fronthaul. Whilst an ultra was heard to have been placed on subjects for a trans Atlantic run in the very low $20,000s, however further details remained under wraps. The Asian arena also saw little signs of a turn around. The south remained rather muted with enquiry keeping pace with tonnage availability. Further north again rates remained under further downward pressure. The 11TC average finished the day down $213 at $12,336.
HANDYSIZE
Extending yesterday’s sentiment, the market continued to face pressure, with the BHSI falling 11 points to 613 and the 7TC average easing by $189 to settle at $11,040. Conditions in the Continent and Mediterranean remained subdued, with minimal visible activity. Although no fixtures were reported, sentiment in the US Gulf appeared steady, supported by a slight pickup in enquiries and signs that available tonnage was beginning to tighten. The South Atlantic recorded some movement and fresh cargo interest, though this was insufficient to materially reduce the regional tonnage overhang. Meanwhile, Asia also faced a slow trading pace with continued downward pressure on rates, and sources noted a slightly longer tonnage list in Southeast Asia.
Atlantic
The Quest (36,903 2011) was reported on subjects for a trip delivery Recalada to the West Coast of South America in the $18,000s. The Agia Irini (38,273 2011) was also placed on subjects for a trip from San Nicolas to Morocco at around $18,500 by Danbulk, though further details were not confirmed. Elsewhere, a 29,000 opening in Tema 5/7 January was heard fixed for a Paranagua to Algeria trip at $15,000. In addition, a 38,000 was fixed from Egypt to Brazil at $6,000, with limited information available.
Asia
The Allegra (40,005 2024), open Kaohsiung, was placed on subjects for a South Korea to Southeast Asia trip with steels at $8,500 by Seanet. The Ocean Fortune (37,595 2014) open Caojing 8/9 January, was reported on subjects for a Southeast Asia trip at around $8,000, though further details were undisclosed. Meanwhile, the Ocean Spring (33,633 2010) open Singapore was placed on subjects for an alumina cargo via Bunbury to China at $7,500. Separately, a 36,000 vessel was heard fixed for delivery in West Australia in the $14,000s, but without additional confirmation.
All the above is given in good faith but wog. E&OE.
Thanks and best regards.
